title: Writ Petition in High Court Bangladesh: A Complete Guide
metaTitle: High Court Writ Petition Expert in Dhaka
metaDescription: Rights violated? Get immediate legal solutions with our Supreme Court lawyer in Dhaka. File your writ petition fast with Advocate Md. Shah Alam.
heroIntro: A writ petition is one of the most powerful legal remedies in Bangladesh — it allows a citizen to challenge illegal government actions, enforce fundamental rights, and seek release from unlawful detention directly in the High Court Division of the Supreme Court.
toc: What is a Writ Petition in Bangladesh?
Grounds for Filing a Writ Petition
How to File: Process and Timeline
When is a Writ NOT the Right Remedy?

sections.h2: What is a Writ Petition in Bangladesh?
sections.content:
A writ petition is an application filed directly in the High Court Division of the Supreme Court of Bangladesh under Article 102 of the Constitution. It is used to challenge the actions of public authorities, enforce fundamental rights guaranteed by Part III of the Constitution, or seek specific legal remedies that ordinary courts cannot provide. Before proceeding, consulting a criminal defense advocate is highly recommended.

Unlike regular civil or criminal cases that start in lower courts, a writ petition begins in the High Court. It requires a specialised Supreme Court lawyer in Bangladesh with experience in constitutional law.

Article 102 of the Bangladesh Constitution empowers the High Court Division to issue writs:

  • On the application of any aggrieved person
  • Against any person performing functions in connection with the affairs of Bangladesh or any unit of local government
  • For enforcement of fundamental rights, or for any of the orders/directions/writs listed in the Article

Article 102 is called the "constitutional court" provision — it makes the High Court the guardian of fundamental rights. No other court has this jurisdiction.

Bangladesh follows the five traditional English writs:

  • Certiorari: Quashes an illegal or ultra vires order/decision of a lower court or public authority.
  • Mandamus: Commands a public authority to perform a legal duty it has failed or refused to perform.
  • Prohibition: Prevents a lower court or tribunal from exceeding its jurisdiction.
  • Quo Warranto: Challenges the right of a person to hold a public office.
  • Habeas Corpus: Secures the release of a person unlawfully detained or imprisoned. The most urgent writ — the court can order production of the detained person within days.

sections.h2: Grounds for Filing a Writ Petition
sections.content:
Common grounds for a writ petition in Bangladesh:

  • Violation of fundamental rights (Articles 27–44 of the Constitution)
  • Unlawful detention without due process
  • Government order passed without authority (ultra vires)
  • Denial of natural justice (no hearing given before adverse order)
  • Arbitrary or discriminatory government action
  • Failure of a regulatory body or authority to perform its statutory duty
  • Unconstitutional legislation

The petitioner must show a clear, specific illegality or rights violation — vague complaints will be dismissed at the initial stage.

sections.h2: How to File: Process and Timeline
sections.content:
Steps to file a writ petition in the High Court Division:

  1. Engage a Supreme Court lawyer to assess the merit and draft the petition.
  2. The petition includes: background facts, reliefs sought, supporting affidavit, annexures (impugned orders, correspondence).
  3. File the petition at the High Court Registry with court fees and affidavits.
  4. The court assigns the case to a Bench and lists it for an initial hearing (Rule).
  5. If the court is satisfied there is merit, it issues a Rule Nisi — calling on the respondent to show cause why the writ should not be made absolute.
  6. The government/respondent files an affidavit-in-opposition.
  7. Hearing on merit — final order (absolute rule, discharge, or other direction).

Urgent cases (habeas corpus, imminent harm) can get an order within 1–3 days. Regular writ cases can take months to years.

Court fees for a writ petition are nominal — typically BDT 200–500 in stamp duty. However, the significant cost is the professional fee of the advocate, which varies by seniority and complexity. A habeas corpus matter or fundamental rights case involving imprisonment can be urgent and expensive. Contact Advocate Md. Shah Alam — an experienced Supreme Court lawyer in Bangladesh — for a realistic fee discussion before proceeding.

faqs.question: Can anyone file a writ petition in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Any "aggrieved person" whose rights have been violated or who is directly affected by an illegal government action can file a writ. In habeas corpus cases, even a relative or friend of the detained person can file on their behalf.

faqs.question: Is there a time limit for filing a writ petition?
faqs.answer: There is no strict limitation period, but undue delay can be grounds for dismissal. Courts expect petitions to be filed promptly after the cause of action arises. Delays must be explained.

faqs.question: Can a writ petition be filed against a private person?
faqs.answer: Generally no. Writs lie against public authorities or persons exercising public functions. For disputes with private parties, ordinary civil or criminal remedies are more appropriate.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a writ and an appeal?
faqs.answer: An appeal challenges a court decision on its merits before a higher court. A writ challenges the legality or jurisdiction of an act/decision, not its factual correctness. Both can sometimes be available for the same situation.

faqs.question: Can a stay order be obtained while the writ is pending?
faqs.answer: Yes. When filing the writ, you can simultaneously apply for a stay/interim relief to prevent the impugned action from taking effect while the case is decided.
